questions.question: Define classical planning and state its three key assumptions.
keyPoints: Classical planning is finding a sequence of actions to achieve a goal from an initial state
Three assumptions: Fully observable (complete knowledge of world state)
Deterministic (actions have predictable outcomes)
Static (world changes only through agent actions)

questions.question: Explain what is meant by a 'fully observable' world in classical planning.
keyPoints: The planner has complete knowledge of the world state
All relevant aspects of the environment are visible
No hidden or unknown variables exist
Current state can be precisely determined at any time

questions.question: What is the difference between action preconditions and action effects?
keyPoints: Preconditions: conditions that must be true BEFORE an action can be applied
Effects: changes to the state AFTER an action is executed
Preconditions determine when an action is applicable
Effects describe what becomes true/false in the new state

questions.question: Describe the components of a formal planning problem.
keyPoints: Initial state: starting configuration of the world
Goal condition: desired state to be achieved
Actions: set of possible actions with preconditions and effects
The problem is solved when a valid plan from initial to goal is found

questions.question: Why is classical planning considered the foundation of automated planning?
keyPoints: It provides simplified but formal model of planning
Makes problem computationally tractable
Concepts form basis for more complex real-world planning
Three key assumptions enable effective algorithm development

questions.question: What is goal-directed behavior in planning agents?
keyPoints: Ability to work backward from desired goal
Find necessary actions to achieve the goal
Not exhaustive trial-and-error approach
More efficient than forward search from initial state

questions.question: Explain classical planning in detail with its assumptions. How do these assumptions make the planning problem tractable?
keyPoints: Classical planning definition: finding sequence of actions to achieve goal from initial state
Three assumptions explained: Fully observable - complete knowledge; Deterministic - predictable outcomes; Static - no external changes
Why assumptions matter: They simplify the problem mathematically
Full observability eliminates uncertainty about current state
Determinism eliminates uncertainty about action outcomes
Static world means no interference during plan execution
Together these enable exact prediction and guarantee that a valid plan will work when executed
Without these assumptions, planning becomes much more complex (requires contingency planning, monitoring, replanning)
These assumptions provide foundation for developing effective planning algorithms

questions.question: Define and explain the components of a planning problem. How does a planning agent solve this problem?
keyPoints: Planning problem formal definition: tuple (Initial state, Goal state, Actions)
Initial state: starting configuration of the world
Goal state: desired conditions to be achieved
Actions: operators with preconditions (when applicable) and effects (what changes)
Plan: sequence of actions from initial to goal state
Planning agent: entity that formulates and executes plans
Agent process: 1) Observe current state 2) Formulate plan to goal 3) Execute plan 4) Update state 5) Repeat until goal achieved
State-space search: exploring possible states via actions to find goal
Solution: valid plan that achieves goal from initial state

questions.question: Discuss the differences between classical planning assumptions and real-world planning scenarios. Why do these differences matter?
keyPoints: Classical assumptions: Fully observable, Deterministic, Static
Real-world: Often partially observable (sensors have limitations), Stochastic/Uncertain actions (unexpected outcomes), Dynamic (world changes during planning/execution)
Examples: Robot in unknown terrain, Business planning with market changes, Autonomous vehicle dealing with traffic
Why differences matter: Classical planning solutions may fail in real scenarios
Classical solutions assume guaranteed success if plan is valid
Real-world requires: Contingency planning, Plan monitoring, Replanning capabilities, Handling unexpected events
Extensions needed: Conditional planning, Probabilistic planning, Execution monitoring, Hierarchical planning
These differences drive research into non-classical planning formalisms

questions.question: Explain the concept of state-space search in classical planning. How does it work and what are its advantages?
keyPoints: State-space: graph where nodes are states, edges are actions
Initial node: initial state
Goal test: checking if goal condition is satisfied
Search: exploring from initial state applying valid actions
Forward search: from initial state toward goal
Backward search: from goal state toward initial state (more efficient often)
Advantages: Systematic exploration, Guaranteed solution if one exists, Foundation for many planning algorithms
Algorithms: BFS, DFS, A* search adapted for planning
State-space explosion: limitation as number of states grows exponentially
Used in practical planners like Graphplan, HSP, FF
